elements.sent-2411-001: This paper provides a comprehensive survey of the latest research on multilingual large language models (MLLMs).
elements.sent-2411-002: MLLMs not only are able to understand and generate language across linguistic boundaries, but also represent an important advancement in artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-003: We first discuss the architecture and pre-training objectives of MLLMs, highlighting the key components and methodologies that contribute to their multilingual capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-004: We then discuss the construction of multilingual pre-training and alignment datasets, underscoring the importance of data quality and diversity in enhancing MLLM performance.
elements.sent-2411-005: An important focus of this survey is on the evaluation of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-006: We present a detailed taxonomy and roadmap covering the assessment of MLLMs' cross-lingual knowledge, reasoning, alignment with human values, safety, interpretability and specialized applications.
elements.sent-2411-007: Specifically, we extensively discuss multilingual evaluation benchmarks and datasets, and explore the use of LLMs themselves as multilingual evaluators.
elements.sent-2411-008: To enhance MLLMs from black to white boxes, we also address the interpretability of multilingual capabilities, cross-lingual transfer and language bias within these models.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we provide a comprehensive review of real-world applications of MLLMs across diverse domains, including biology, medicine, computer science, mathematics and law.
elements.sent-2411-010: We showcase how these models have driven innovation and improvements in these specialized fields while also highlighting the challenges and opportunities in deploying MLLMs within diverse language communities and application scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-011: We listed the paper related in this survey and publicly available at a Github repository.\footnote{\url{https://github.com/tjunlp-lab/Awesome-Multilingual-LLMs-Papers.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Scientific progress and technological innovation are a key driving force of human society, and the recent advancement of language science and technology has been part of them.
elements.sent-2411-013: While being a crucial mean for people to communicate, language has enhanced knowledge accumulation and cultural inheritance.
elements.sent-2411-014: And given the multiple languages in the world, efforts towards economic, cultural, political and other forms of communication have always require multilingual understanding.
elements.sent-2411-015: With the deepening of globalization, the development of language technology and the search for multilingual comprehension has only accelerated.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the current information society and Artificial Intelligence (AI) age, we are at an unprecedented juncture of knowledge availability and cognitive revolution.
elements.sent-2411-017: Being a key factor underlying this transformative shift, generative Large Language Models (LLMs) are fostering a new dimension of intelligence with their extraordinary language processing capabilities — the ``intelligence of language'' \citep{chang2024survey .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Though still in its infancy, this form of intelligence is sweeping across every domain of scientific research and technological innovation.
elements.sent-2411-019: We are witnessing the emergence of a new level of intelligence capabilities that has the potential to transcend human cognitive limits, specially when this encompasses different languages and cultures.
elements.sent-2411-020: Therefore, endowing LLMs with multilingual capabilities has become a crucial endeavour towards realizing their full potential.
elements.sent-2411-021: A Multilingual LLM (MLLM) is an LLM that seeks to learn such capabilities across multiple languages by means of a single model.
elements.sent-2411-022: Efforts to equip AI models with multilingual capabilities can be traced back to machine translation systems based on statistical methods, such as IBM's Candide system \citep{hutchins-1999-retrospect , and even further back, to rule-based systems, relying on manually designed correspondences between expressions of different languages.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Over a decade ago, with the rapid advancement of computational power and the arrival of the big data era, neural network-based end-to-end multilingual language models began to emerge.
elements.sent-2411-024: In 2014, \citet{DBLP:conf/emnlp/ChoMGBBSB14 proposed an attention-based neural machine translation model, greatly improving translation quality.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In 2016, Google researchers introduced the GNMT neural machine translation system, built on a large-scale corpus, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple language pairs.
elements.sent-2411-026: These early multilingual models typically required separate training and optimization for each language pair, lacking scalability and generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-027: Seeking to address this bottleneck, a new generation of pre-trained multilingual language models emerged.
elements.sent-2411-028: In 2019, \citet{DBLP:conf/naacl/DevlinCLT19 introduced the BERT model, which achieved stronger cross-lingual transfer abilities through pre-training on large-scale multilingual corpora.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Since then, a series of multilingual pre-trained language models, including XLM \citep{DBLP:conf/nips/ConneauL19 , mBART \citep{DBLP:journals/tacl/LiuGGLEGLZ20 , among many others, have been proposed, broadening the potential of multilingual modeling.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Researchers no longer viewed multilingual processing as an extension of machine translation but rather as a holistic ability to understand and handle meaning across languages.
elements.sent-2411-031: To improve the training efficiency of models on massive multilingual data, innovative training objectives and architectures have been continuously proposed, such as the denoising autoencoding of XLM-R \citep{DBLP:conf/acl/ConneauKGCWGGOZ20 and the multitask architecture of mT5 \citep{DBLP:conf/naacl/XueCRKASBR21 , just to mention a couple of examples, among countless others.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The rapid adoption of ChatGPT (OpenAI, 2022), attracting over 100 million users in just two months, has highlighted the transformative potential of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-033: Their capabilities, including natural text generation, code generation and tool usage, coupled with their surprisingly strong multilingual abilities, have both triggered enthusiasm and critical questions \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-12204,DBLP:journals/bjmc/TarsTF22 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The path forward for MLLMs is not without challenges.
elements.sent-2411-035: While many surveys have explored specific aspects of MLLMs \citep{xu2024survey,qin2024multilingual , such as their training data, architecture or applications, a comprehensive examination of their multilingual capabilities, limitations, and challenges is lacking.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Additionally, critical issues related to responsible AI, such as fairness and toxicity, have not been adequately addressed in that context \citep{DBLP:conf/acl/PhilippyGH23,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-16438 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The present survey aims to fill this gap by providing a comprehensive survey of the research on MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-038: We will analyze the specific challenges MLLMs face in handling linguistic diversity, thus including non-English and low-resource languages, and explore data construction strategies, model training and fine-tuning approaches, as well as core responsible AI issues.
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, we will delve into the practical challenges of deploying MLLMs in real-world language communities and application domains.
elements.sent-2411-040: This survey seeks thus to provide essential insights for navigating the evolving landscape of MLLMs and their impact on a global scale.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[scale=0.51]{latex/taxonomy_fix.pdf} \caption{Our proposed taxonomy of major categories and sub-categories of MLLMs.} \label{taxonomy} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-041: We will discuss data construction strategies, model training and fine-tuning approaches, and core Responsible AI issues such as fairness, bias and toxicity in the context of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-042: We will also analyze the specific challenges MLLMs encounter in handling linguistic diversity, along with relevant evaluation methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-043: As the performance of MLLMs continues to improve, leveraging them for developing multilingual language technologies globally has become increasingly viable.
elements.sent-2411-044: This survey aims thus to provide researchers in linguistics and artificial intelligence with a cutting-edge perspective on the development of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-045: We will analyze the opportunities and challenges that MLLMs face in towards realizing the full potential of language intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-046: Our goal is to facilitate more inclusive and responsible language technology development, ensuring that these powerful tools are accessible and beneficial to diverse language communities worldwide.
elements.sent-2411-047: By exploring the intricacies of multilingualism, encompassing linguistic diversity, language variations, and multilingual knowledge transfer, we strive to bridge the gap between the current state of LLM research and the urgent need for equitable and effective language understanding across all languages.
elements.sent-2411-048: Through this comprehensive examination of MLLMs, we aim to contribute for language intelligence to empower global communication, to foster cultural understanding and to unlock the potential of diverse knowledge systems.
elements.sent-2411-049: To provide a comprehensive overview of the rapidly evolving area of Multilingual Large Language Models, the present survey adopts a structured taxonomy, organizing the research landscape into six fundamental and interconnected domains.
elements.sent-2411-050: Beginning with the foundational element of multilingual data, this survey considers diverse sources like web crawls, books, and code repositories.
elements.sent-2411-051: It then delves into the neural architecture choices for building effective MLLMs, analyzing the strengths and weaknesses of common architectures like decoder-only and encoder-decoder.
elements.sent-2411-052: Building upon these foundations, this survey addresses the methodologies for pre-training and fine-tuning MLLMs, discussing various objectives like masked language modeling and translation language modeling, as well as fine-tuning techniques like instruction tuning and preference tuning.
elements.sent-2411-053: The crucial aspect of evaluating MLLMs performance is addressed through a comprehensive review of benchmarks and datasets, emphasizing the importance of balanced evaluation across diverse languages, including low-resource languages.
elements.sent-2411-054: This paper then takes into account the ``black box'' dimension of MLLMs and discusses interpretability techniques to understand how these models achieve their multilingual capabilities and how to identify and mitigate potential linguistic biases \citep{gurgurov2024multilingual,blevins2024breaking,nezhad2024drives,kojima2024multilingual,liu2024unraveling .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Finally, the survey showcases diverse real-world applications of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-056: Our overarching objective is to address these six fundamental domains, as illustrated in Figure \ref{taxonomy .}
elements.sent-2411-057: The next Section \ref{Architectures discusses the key architectural components and pre-training objectives that contribute to the development and training of MLLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-058: MLLMs typically adopt either the decoder-only or encoder-decoder architecture.
elements.sent-2411-059: The acquisition of multilingual knowledge comes from training on multilingual corpora.
elements.sent-2411-060: The main architectural difference between multilingual and monolingual LLMs lies in the vocabulary, which needs to be larger in MLLMs to address the out-of-vocabulary issue.
elements.sent-2411-061: In terms of pre-training objectives, MLLMs use similar techniques to monolingual models, such as masked language modeling, but also explore novel objectives like translation language modeling to enhance cross-lingual abilities.
elements.sent-2411-062: Section \ref{Multilingual Corpora discusses the training data for MLLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The pre-training stage requires large amounts of unlabeled text data, while the alignment stage requires supervised fine-tuning with parallel data and reinforcement learning with feedback data.
elements.sent-2411-064: In terms of pre-training data, we summarize the characteristics of various web data, book data, code data, and academic paper data.
elements.sent-2411-065: Regarding parallel data, we detail the generation methods for supervised fine-tuning data, including human-generated and model-assisted generation, as well as the process of collecting reinforcement learning feedback data.
elements.sent-2411-066: The pre-training of MLLMs is discussed in Section \ref{Pre-training , where the process of data curation is examined, together with the pre-training objectives that should be set and the pre-training strategies that have been explored in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Section \ref{Multilingual Tuning of LLMs presents strategies for multilingual tuning, with the goal of adapting the general capabilities of multilingual LLMs to specific objectives.}
elements.sent-2411-068: It begins by discussing approaches that extend LLMs tuning directly into multilingual contexts, with a focus on multilingual data collection and cross-lingual transfer.
elements.sent-2411-069: Additionally, it introduces strategies to further enhance cross-lingual alignment during multilingual tuning.
elements.sent-2411-070: Finally, it addresses specialized tuning techniques for the enhancement of specific multilingual capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-071: Next, in Section \ref{Multilingual Evaluation of LLMs , we address the evaluation of MLLMs by extensively discussing the evaluation, from multilingual tokenization to the datasets and including benchmarks used to evaluate MLLMs' capabilities across diverse tasks in a multilingual context.}
elements.sent-2411-072: We further discuss the evaluation methods for determining the multilingualism of MLLMs and explore the use of MLLMs themselves in evaluating MLLMs' performance.
elements.sent-2411-073: In seeking to transform an MLLM from a black box to a white box, Section \ref{Interpretability of MLLMs addresses the core question of how the model represents multilingual capacities.}
elements.sent-2411-074: It also tackles advanced issues such as cross-lingual transfer and the causes of language bias.
elements.sent-2411-075: In section \ref{Application of MLLMs , we undertake a thorough investigation into the evolutionary pathway and the latest breakthroughs of MLLMs spanning diverse disciplinary realms, with a particular emphasis on their real-world implementations and applications.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Overall, our aim is to address and help to clarify the following fundamental questions:.
elements.sent-2411-077: What are the capabilities of MLLMs?.
elements.sent-2411-078: What is the language boundary of MLLMs?.
elements.sent-2411-079: What factors must be taken into account when constructing and tuning MLLMs?.
elements.sent-2411-080: How to evaluate the multilingual transfer capabilities of MLLMs?.
elements.sent-2411-081: As large language models can excel in English, their performance varies though across the other languages.
elements.sent-2411-082: Accordingly, there is a growing need to advance multilingual large language models in order to ensure a balanced performance across a broader range of languages.
elements.sent-2411-083: Against this context, the goal of the present section is to provide a thorough examination of both LLMs and MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-084: We will discuss the architecture of these models, typically based on the Transformer model \citep{vaswani2017attention , as well as other architectures that may have the potential to challenge such supremacy of Transformers.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Organized according to the major families of architectures, the deployment of major MLLMs along the last few years are summarized in Figure \ref{S3 .}
elements.sent-2411-086: MLLMs tend typically to adopt the same architecture as monolingual LLMs, even in the era of pre-trained language models \citep{bendale2024sutra .}
elements.sent-2411-087: However, the finer details of the Transformer architecture can vary significantly when building a top performing language model, which inherently affects the performance and stability of pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-088: In an initial period, the preferred architecture was usually either the encoder-only model \citep{bert, XLM, XLM-R, erine or the encoder-decoder model \citep{BART, T5, mT5, NLLB , both of which typically utilize non-causal masked attention to perform denoising tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Following the subsequent success of GPT models \citep{GPT3, InstructGPT , the preferred approach shifted to the decoder-only model, which excel in generation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Useful pre-training of these models involves training with a substantial number of parameters, say over 7 billion, on a massive amount of tokens, over 1 trillion.
elements.sent-2411-091: This process requires extensive GPU memory and time, which represents a significant cost.
elements.sent-2411-092: To address these challenges and enhance the performance of pre-trained models, various modifications have been made to the vanilla Transformers, often related to the self-attention mechanism, layer normalization, activation functions and position embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-093: LLaMA \citep{llama-1 is one of the first open foundation models that paved the way for the success of open source MLLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Despite using the vanilla Transformer architecture, LLaMA introduces significant modifications.
elements.sent-2411-095: It adopts pre-normalization like GPT-3 \citep{GPT3 , and uses RMSNorm \citep{rmsnorm instead of the traditional layer normalization, which eliminates the shift operation of layer normalization and improves FLOPs.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Additionally, LLaMA chooses SwiGLU, following PaLM \citep{chowdhery2022palmscalinglanguagemodeling , to enhance performance, and Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) \citep{rope , following GPTNeo \citep{gpt-neo , instead of standard absolute position embeddings to support long-context extension.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: LLaMA also removes the linear bias of the feed-forward networks and self-attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-098: This design approach has been widely adopted by successors such as Yi \citep{Yi , Deepseek-LLM \citep{DeepseekLLM , InternLM \citep{internlm .}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: For example, Qwen1.0 \citep{qwen retains the bias in self-attention, and Gemma uses GeGLU \citep{Gemma instead of SwiGLU.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Falcon \citep{falcon and BaiChuan-13B \citep{baichuan uses ALiBi \citep{ALiBi instead of RoPE for position embeddings.}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: To decrease memory usage during inference, Grouped Query Attention (GQA) \citep{GQA has been implemented in many LLMs, including LLaMA-2 \citep{llama-2 , Yi \citep{Yi , DeepSeek-LLM \citep{DeepseekLLM , InternLM 2 \citep{internlm2 , and OpenELM \citep{OpenELM .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Compared to Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHA), GQA groups multiple queries and uses a single key and value head, reducing memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-104: MLLMs like BLOOM \citep{BLOOM and PolyLM~\citep{tuning-7 use the GeLU activation function and vanilla layer normalization, as well as ALiBi instead of RoPE for position embeddings.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: These architectural differences are not specific to language, but rather general improvements.
elements.sent-2411-106: BLOOM also expanded the vocabulary size to 250K to reduce the risk of under-segmenting for multilingual purposes, whereas other monolingual LLMs from the same period used much smaller vocabulary sizes, typically around 32K.
elements.sent-2411-107: Another MLLM FuxiTranyu~\citep{fuxitranyu makes a balance between performance and training efficiency, using GeLU activation function and RMSNorm.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Similar to BLOOM, FuxiTranyu expands the vocabulary size to 250K to fit the multilingual corpora.
elements.sent-2411-109: Researchers have also proposed new alternatives to MHA or GQA.
elements.sent-2411-110: For instance, the Mistral-7B model \citep{mistral adopts Sliding Window Attention (SWA), and DeepSeek-V2 \citep{DeepseekV2 proposes Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA).}}
elements.sent-2411-111: These variants aim to reduce the KV cache during inference.
elements.sent-2411-112: The Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture is an effective approach for scaling model parameters while keeping the FLOPs similar to their dense counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-113: Research on MoE models has shown promising results across multiple tasks \citep{Outrageously .}
elements.sent-2411-114: OpenMoE \citep{OpenMoE is one of the first attempts to build an open-sourced LLM adopting an MoE approach, with parameters ranging from 650M to 34B, pre-trained on 1T tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Unlike Switch Transformer or GShard \citep{switchtransformer, GShard , which replace the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) module with MoE, OpenMoE interleaves the MoE module with the FFN module.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Mixtral-8x7B \citep{Mixtral , in turn, is an outstanding LLM that pushes forward the experimentation with MoE.}
elements.sent-2411-117: It adopts an architecture similar to the dense Mistral-7B model, replacing the FFN module with the MoE module.
elements.sent-2411-118: Mixtral-8x7B contains 8 experts per layer and uses a top-2 routing mechanism to select experts for each token.
elements.sent-2411-119: Xai's Grok-1 model\footnote{https://x.ai/blog/grok follows this setup, scaling the parameters to 314B.}
elements.sent-2411-120: DeepSeek MoE \citep{DeepSeekMoE employs a similar architecture but with a fine-grained expert design.}
elements.sent-2411-121: The original $N$ experts are segmented into $mN$ experts and correspondingly routed to $mK$ experts instead of $K$ experts to achieve a more flexible combination of activated experts.
elements.sent-2411-122: Additionally, it leaves $K_s$ experts as shared experts, leading to a final number of routed experts of $mK - K_s$ while $mK$ experts are activated.
elements.sent-2411-123: Using this MoE architecture, performance similar to their dense counterparts is achieved, with the same total number of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-124: The Qwen1.5-MoE series \citep{qwen_moe follows DeepSeek MoE's successful approach, using 4 shared experts and 4 of 60 activated experts.}
elements.sent-2411-125: DBRX \citep{DBRX , in turn, uses a similar idea to build a fine-grained MoE architecture with 16 experts, activating 4 of them.}
elements.sent-2411-126: These fine-grained MoE shows promising results compared to Mixtral-8x7B and Grok-1.
elements.sent-2411-127: However, they do not use shared experts.
elements.sent-2411-128: In summary, the MoE approach provides an effective way to scale the parameters of MLLMs, and fine-grained expert design leads to surprisingly good performance compared to dense counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-129: In the context of multilingual models, the main issue with model architecture is often its limited capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-130: It remains an open question to be examined whether a fine-grained MoE architecture would significantly boost the performance of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-131: Despite the supremacy of the Transformer architecture for MLLMs, it has a significant drawback: its time complexity scales quadratically with the sequence length.
elements.sent-2411-132: Competitors to the transformer often propose architectures with linear time complexity.
elements.sent-2411-133: RWKV \citep{rwkv is a variant of the Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) architecture that stacks residual blocks with time-mixing and channel-mixing modules.}
elements.sent-2411-134: RWKV has scaled to 7 billion parameters and matches Transformer performance on multiple benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-135: Mamba \citep{mamba follows previous state-space models like S4 and H3 \citep{gu2022efficientlymodelinglongsequences, fu2023hungryhungryhipposlanguage , adopting the state-space architecture while scaling parameters to 1.3 billion.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: It has shown promising results on multiple validation sets using perplexity as the metric, although whether it can scale much larger remains unknown.
elements.sent-2411-137: Jamba \citep{jamba can be seen as a successful fusion of Mamba and Transformers, stacking Mamba layers, Mamba with MoE layers, and Transformer layers together.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Jamba inherits the high-quality output of transformers and the high throughput of Mamba, demonstrating remarkable performance on several benchmarks compared to LLaMA-2 70B and Mixtral 8x7B, with a total of 52 billion parameters and 12 billion activated parameters.
elements.sent-2411-139: Training MLLMs typically involves three major stages: pre-training, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF).
elements.sent-2411-140: Pre-training requires vast amounts of unlabeled, raw text, while SFT and RLHF occur during the alignment phase.
elements.sent-2411-141: The GPT-3 \citep{DBLP:conf/nips/BrownMRSKDNSSAA20 , based on the transformer architecture, demonstrates strong contextual learning abilities with pre-training using only large amounts of unlabeled data.}
elements.sent-2411-142: However, it still shows significant gaps in areas such as value alignment.
elements.sent-2411-143: The powerful capabilities of ChatGPT arise from quality data used in supervised fine-tuning and intense RLHF \citep{DBLP:conf/nips/Ouyang0JAWMZASR22 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Subsequently, skillful chat models such as the Qwen-Chat series \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-16609 , LLaMA-Chat series \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2302-13971 , and Baichuan 2-Chat series \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-10305 have emerged, all having undergone both SFT and RLHF.}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: Consequently, gathering, preparing, curating and handling data deserves and has gained substantial research, as witnessed by the expanding ELRA Language Resources Association's LREC conferences,\footnote{https://www.elra.info/elra-events/lrec/ among others.}
elements.sent-2411-146: In this section, we will introduce the pre-training and alignment data for MLLMs separately.
elements.sent-2411-147: For the alignment phase, we will discuss SFT data and RLHF data in detail.
elements.sent-2411-148: Pre-training is the most time-consuming and resource-intensive stage during a typical MLLMs training.
elements.sent-2411-149: Compared to alignment data, the amount of pre-training data tends to be much larger.
elements.sent-2411-150: Qwen \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-16609 has undergone pre-training on 2 to 3 trillion tokens, Baichuan 2 \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-10305 on 2.6 trillion tokens, and the LLaMA 3\footnote{\url{https://ai.meta.com/blog/meta-llama-3 model on 15 trillion tokens.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: To ensure that the model can learn and generalize across input data during the pre-training stage, the pre-training data encompasses a wide range of fields and sources, and data filtering methods should be employed to ensure its quality.
elements.sent-2411-152: For MLLMs, a rich collection of multilingual data enables the model to learn connections between languages, and generalize across them, thereby enhancing its multilingual capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-153: In this section, we will focus on multilingual pre-training data sets.. \newcommand{\gou {\ding{52.}}}

elements.sent-2411-188: In this section, we introduce the datasets for so-called alignment.
elements.sent-2411-189: Alignment is an important research topic as pre-training enables models to acquire knowledge, while alignment allows models to follow instructions and align to human preferences and ethics.
elements.sent-2411-190: The alignment process for MLLMs is usually divided into two stages: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF).
elements.sent-2411-191: SFT involves inputting diverse instructions and producing the respective outputs that align with human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-192: Two data-related factors influence the effectiveness of instruction fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-193: First, data quality: high-quality data is more effective than larger quantities of data \citep{llama-2 .}
elements.sent-2411-194: Second, the diversity of instructions: a richer variety of instruction types tend to better help the alignment of the models.
elements.sent-2411-195: The development of SFT datasets can be categorized into LLM-generated and human-annotated data.
elements.sent-2411-196: According to LLaMA 2 \citep{llama-2 , synthesized SFT data is competitive with human-annotated SFT data.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Consequently, a significant portion of SFT data is actually derived from model-assisted generation or entirely model-generated.
elements.sent-2411-198: This approach allows for the reallocation of substantial human annotation costs to other alignment data, such as RLHF.
elements.sent-2411-199: We classify the data into three categories based on the method of synthesis: AI-Generated, Manually-Created, and mixed, and provide an overview of the SFT datasets.
elements.sent-2411-200: AI-generated data is produced by MLLMs like GPT-4\footnote{https://openai.com/index/gpt-4/ or others, sometimes using seed instructions or prompts provided by humans.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Manually-created data is constructed entirely by humans.
elements.sent-2411-202: Mixed datasets include portions that are manually-created, AI-generated, and data obtained by applying instruction templates to traditional NLP datasets.
elements.sent-2411-203: A summary of multilingual SFT datasets is shown in Table \ref{tab:sft-data .}

elements.sent-2411-233: Thanks to the results obtained by the open-source community, the techniques underlying the pre-training of MLLMs have become increasingly more transparent.
elements.sent-2411-234: The primary techniques in the pre-training phase of MLLMs revolve around the collection and pre-processing of multilingual data, which takes a dominant role.
elements.sent-2411-235: Pre-training objectives and strategies are also crucial to their eventual performance.
elements.sent-2411-236: Research has demonstrated that a high-quality, de-duplicated pre-training dataset is essential for the enhanced performance of pre-trained LLMs \citep{Yi,deduplicating-lee .}
elements.sent-2411-237: Given that a substantial portion of pre-training data is sourced from the web, ensuring the quality and safety of this data is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-238: The process of developing such dataset for a well-performing LLM can be organized into five parts: language identification, quality filtering, safety filtering, deduplication, and up-sampling of certain domains.
elements.sent-2411-239: Language identification is crucial for subsequent filtering methods since heuristic rule filters are typically based on the statistical information of the dataset, which can vary significantly across different languages.
elements.sent-2411-240: For quality filtering, combining filters based on heuristic rules with machine learned filters improves data quality as they permit to take into consideration both syntactic and semantic aspects \citep{Yi, DeepseekLLM, internlm2 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: Heuristic rule filters often involve filtering documents based on pre-defined block lists, such as URLs, and the statistical information of documents, including character/token/digit/symbol ratios and the frequency of repeated words/n-grams/paragraphs \citep{Yi, internlm2, OpenELM .}
elements.sent-2411-242: These rules are usually designed based on observations of the data.
elements.sent-2411-243: For instance, BLOOM \citep{BLOOM defines a series of indicators to filter out low-quality data for each language, with indicators selected by fluent speakers of each language.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Learned filters typically involve task-specific trained models.
elements.sent-2411-245: Examples include using KenLM\footnote{https://github.com/kpu/kenlm to measure document perplexity or trained quality models to score data quality.}
elements.sent-2411-246: For instance, \citet{internlm2 organize human annotators to label document quality and fine-tune BERT models to serve as filters.}
elements.sent-2411-247: LLaMA 3 \citep{LLaMA-3 finds that using LLaMA 2 to identify high-quality data yield excellent results, and leverages it to generate high-quality text data.}
elements.sent-2411-248: To enhance the safety of pre-training data, documents containing unsafe content must be filtered out.
elements.sent-2411-249: Like quality filters, safety filters are composed of manually designed heuristic rules and machine learned filters, targeting, for instance, personally identifiable information (PII), toxicity or unwanted words or domains \citep{Yi, internlm2, qwen, llama-2, Gemma .}
elements.sent-2411-250: As another example, \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2405-04434 removes also contentious content from the pre-training corpus to mitigate cultural bias in the data.}
elements.sent-2411-251: Turning to text deduplication, it significantly impacts LLM performance.
elements.sent-2411-252: Current deduplication methods include exact-match deduplication and fuzzy-match deduplication, using algorithms like MinHash and LSH \citep{Yi, DeepseekLLM, qwen, internlm, LLaMA-3 .}
elements.sent-2411-253: Effective deduplication ensures that redundant data does not skew the training process.
elements.sent-2411-254: Some dataset were also subject to up-sampling methods for specific domains or data sources to enhance their diversity \citep{DeepseekLLM, qwen, llama-2 .}
elements.sent-2411-255: This approach ensures that the pre-training dataset represents a broad range of linguistic and contextual variations.
elements.sent-2411-256: Most popular MLLMs have increasingly adopted the decoder-only architecture and causal mask attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-257: This focus has led to a corresponding focus in terms of pre-training objectives, moving from masked language modeling to next token prediction.
elements.sent-2411-258: Under this pre-training objective, MLLMs are trained autoregressively to predict the next token given the current tokens, which can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = -\sum_t^Tp(x_{t+1} | x_{<t}; \theta)\end{equation} where $x$ stands for a sequence of tokens, $t$ represents each time step, and $\theta$ the parameters of the model.
elements.sent-2411-259: Besides the next token prediction objectives, masked language modeling tasks can also be used to train LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-260: UL2 \citep{UL2 proposes using a combination of denoising objectives for pre-training.}
elements.sent-2411-261: It includes R-Denoiser, S-Denoiser, and X-Denoiser.
elements.sent-2411-262: R-Denoiser is the standard span corruption introduced in T5 \citep{T5 , typically masking 15\% of the input tokens in spans of 2-5 tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-263: S-Denoiser is akin to prefix language modeling, partitioning the input sequence into two sub-sequences corresponding to source and target.
elements.sent-2411-264: X-Denoiser is similar to R-Denoiser but with longer spans and a higher corruption rate.
elements.sent-2411-265: By incorporating these denoisers, UL2 pre-training objectives can be applied to any model architecture.
elements.sent-2411-266: It has been successfully applied to PaLM-2, showing promising results.
elements.sent-2411-267: OpenMoE \citep{OpenMoE also experimented with UL2 as the pre-training objective but found that the model's improvement slowed beyond the early training stage.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Consequently, its training reverted to next token prediction.
elements.sent-2411-269: To fully utilize computing resources during the pre-training stage, packing \citep{T5 is used instead of padding, as padding introduces meaningless computation.}
elements.sent-2411-270: Packing involves collecting different sequences into a single document and then truncating it into multiple smaller segments of a predefined sequence length.
elements.sent-2411-271: However, this method disregards sentence semantics, potentially truncating sentences in the middle or concatenating multiple irrelevant sequences into a single segment.
elements.sent-2411-272: Going beyond a single pre-training stage, many MLLMs incorporate a multi-stage pre-training strategy aimed at extending the context length \citep{internlm2, DeepseekV2 .}
elements.sent-2411-273: The quadratic complexity of self-attention computation related to context length results in a significant increase in both computation and memory costs.
elements.sent-2411-274: And implementing a multi-stage pre-training with an expanded long context window has proven to be an effective solution, as this stage requires fewer tokens than the initial stage.
elements.sent-2411-275: For example, InternLM 2 \citep{internlm2 dedicates 90\% of total training steps to a 4096 context length, while the remaining 9\% of training steps use a 32K context length.}
elements.sent-2411-276: In addition to the stage that extends context length, MiniCPM \citep{MiniCPM proposes another pre-training strategy to enhance MLLM performance on downstream tasks, called the warmup-stable-decay learning rate scheduler (WSD LRS).}
elements.sent-2411-277: The warmup and stable stages are the same as in conventional pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-278: In the decay stage, the learning rate rapidly decreases from the maximum to the minimum.
elements.sent-2411-279: The loss curve demonstrates that this strategy achieves significantly better performance compared to the original pre-training strategy.
elements.sent-2411-280: MiniCPM suggests that 10\% of training steps dedicated to the decay stage is sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-281: There are over 7,000 languages spoken on the planet, yet most existing LLMs are primarily English-based or have limited multilingual capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-282: This hampers access to cutting-edge Artificial Intelligence for non-English speakers and poses the risk of cultural uniformisation and the loss of linguistic diversity.
elements.sent-2411-283: To democratize LLMs across languages, a number of efforts have sought to extend tuning of MLLMs to multilingual scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-284: As summarized in Figure \ref{fig:multilingual tuning , in this section, we start by introducing common tuning strategies to adapt the general capabilities of LLMs to specific objectives.}
elements.sent-2411-285: Then, we present two common multilingual tuning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-286: The first is direct multilingual tuning, which simply expands tuning data to multiple languages; the second focuses on cross-lingual alignment during multilingual tuning, transferring capabilities from the pivot language (primarily English) to other languages.
elements.sent-2411-287: We will also discuss specific tuning methods used to enhance particular multilingual capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-288: Here we introduce foundational tuning techniques for adapting the general capabilities of MLLMs to specific objectives.
elements.sent-2411-289: These techniques also serve as the basis for multilingual tuning.
elements.sent-2411-290: They are: instruction tuning, preference tuning, and continual pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-291: In general, MLLMs are trained using a self-supervised learning objective, which involves predicting a token on the basis of the preceding or surrounding tokens \citep{DBLP:conf/nips/BrownMRSKDNSSAA20,DBLP:journals/jmlr/ChowdheryNDBMRBCSGSSTMRBTSPRDHPBAI23,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2104-12369,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2205-01068,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2211-05100,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2112-11446,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2302-13971,shaham2024multilingual .}
elements.sent-2411-292: When a prompt is entered, the MLLMs generate a completion for that prompt that is contextually appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-293: However, despite the appropriateness of these completions, they may not always align with human preferences, thereby limiting the practical applications of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-294: To address this issue, instruction tuning has been proposed, which aims to enhance the alignment of generated responses with human preferences and has become the most widely method adopted to fine-tune MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-295: Instruction tuning is a technique designed to bridge the gap between the next-token prediction objective of MLLMs during pre-training and the goal of providing desired responses for humans in practical scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-296: Unlike the pre-training stage, which typically involves training the LLMs on document corpora, instruction tuning involves training the models with instructional prompts and corresponding completions.
elements.sent-2411-297: The pioneering work on instruction tuning involves fine-tuning MLLMs on instructions paraphrased from samples of various NLP tasks, which substantially enhances the performance of MLLMs on unseen tasks \citep{DBLP:conf/iclr/WeiBZGYLDDL22,DBLP:conf/iclr/SanhWRBSACSRDBX22 .}
elements.sent-2411-298: However, fine-tuning MLLMs solely on data from NLP tasks can result in models that excel at completing these tasks rather than effectively interacting with humans.
elements.sent-2411-299: Notably, \citet{DBLP:conf/nips/Ouyang0JAWMZASR22,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2204-05862,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2112-00861 fine-tuned MLLMs with instruction data that more closely align with everyday human instructions.}
elements.sent-2411-300: This type of instruction data can be authored by either humans or LLMs themselves \citep{DBLP:conf/acl/WangKMLSKH23,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2304-12244,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2306-08568 .}
elements.sent-2411-301: A critical difference between pre-training and instruction tuning is that the latter employs a prompt template that specifies the roles and tasks for MLLMs, which can be populated with samples to conduct the tuning process.
elements.sent-2411-302: Additionally, while pre-training typically computes loss over all tokens in a training sample, instruction tuning can ignore the loss of prompt tokens \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2308-06259 .}
elements.sent-2411-303: However, recent studies suggest that optimizing the loss over both prompt tokens and response tokens yields superior performance compared to optimizing the loss over response tokens alone \citep{shi2024instruction,huertaenochian2024instruction .}
elements.sent-2411-304: This data is generated by annotating the more preferred completion between two options \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2204-05862 or by ranking completions according to human preference \citep{DBLP:conf/nips/Ouyang0JAWMZASR22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-305: Currently, two primary techniques are used for preference tuning: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1909-08593,DBLP:conf/nips/StiennonO0ZLVRA20,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2112-09332,DBLP:conf/nips/Ouyang0JAWMZASR22 and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) \citep{DBLP:conf/nips/RafailovSMMEF23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-306: RLHF comprises three essential steps for training LLMs: (1) training LLMs with instruction tuning, also known as supervised fine-tuning; (2) training a reward model using preference data to predict human preferences; and (3) applying reinforcement learning to further train MLLMs from step 1, thereby enhancing the alignment between MLLMs and human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-307: In step 2, the trained reward model produces a scalar reward to quantify the degree of human preference for given completions, where a higher reward denotes greater alignment with human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-308: The parameters $\theta$ of the reward model are optimized based on the assumption that the preference data used for training are sampled from a preference function that adheres to the Bradley-Terry model \citep{bradley1952rank .}
elements.sent-2411-309: Let $x$ denote the prompt, and let $y_{w}$ and $y_{l}$ denote the ``win'' and ``lose'' responses, respectively, where ``win'' and ``lose'' represent the preferred and less preferred responses as chosen by humans.
elements.sent-2411-310: Let $r$ denote the preference function that maps a prompt and its corresponding completion to a scalar value, reflecting human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-311: The sigmoid function is denoted by $\sigma \left ( \cdot \right )$.
elements.sent-2411-312: The notation $y_{w \succ y_{l}$ indicates that $y_{w}$ is more preferred than $y_{l}$.}
elements.sent-2411-313: According to the Bradley-Terry model, the probability that $y_w$ is preferred over $y_l$ can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}P\left ( y_{w} \succ y_{l} | x \right ) = \frac{exp \left ( r\left ( x, y_{w} \right ) \right )}{exp\left ( r\left ( x, y_{w} \right ) + exp\left ( r\left ( x, y_{l} \right ) \right ) \right )} \notag = \sigma \left ( r\left ( x, y_{w} \right ) - r\left ( x, y_{l} \right ) \right )\end{equation} Consequently, the reward model $r_{\theta}$ can be trained to minimize the following objective: \begin{equation}\mathcal L\left ( r_{\theta} \right ) = - \mathrm{log} \sigma \left ( r_{\theta} \left ( x, y_{w} \right ) - r_{\theta} \left ( x, y_{l} \right ) \right )\end{equation} All parameters of the reward model, with the exception of the final embedding layer, can be initialized from either pre-trained MLLMs \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2310-01377,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2311-04919,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2310-06452 or instruction-tuned LLMs \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-09288 .}}

elements.sent-2411-322: Pre-trained MLLMs can be further adapted to specific objectives through methods such as instruction tuning and preference tuning.
elements.sent-2411-323: Continual pre-training serves as an intermediary step between the initial pre-training and task-specific fine-tuning, adapting MLLMs' general knowledge to specific domains or integrating entirely new knowledge~\citep{cpt_1,mu2024revealing,li2024comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-324: This process enhances the adaptability of MLLMs to specific tasks, thereby improving task performance.
elements.sent-2411-325: The training objective during continual pre-training is next-token prediction, similar to that in the pre-training stage.
elements.sent-2411-326: However, a significant challenge in continual pre-training is catastrophic forgetting~\citep{cpt_1 , which occurs when MLLMs are optimized on the target data that are domain-specific or entirely new compared to the pre-training data.}
elements.sent-2411-327: In essence, this phenomenon arises from the distributional differences between the target data and the original pre-training data.
elements.sent-2411-328: Catastrophic forgetting is a critical issue because it not only erases previously acquired knowledge but also hinders effective knowledge transfer.
elements.sent-2411-329: To mitigate catastrophic forgetting, common approaches include replay, regularization, and architecture-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-330: Specifically, replay-based methods involve incorporating samples from the original training data alongside new target data~\citep{cpt_2,cpt_3,cpt_4,cpt_5,cpt_6,cpt_7 .}
elements.sent-2411-331: Regularization-based methods impose constraints to minimize discrepancies between updated and original model parameters~\citep{cpt_8,cpt_9 , while architecture-based methods primarily focus on parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques~\citep{cpt_10,cpt_11,cpt_12,cpt_13,cpt_14,cpt_15,cpt_16 .}}
elements.sent-2411-332: To democratize MLLMs across languages, many studies directly extend the tuning of MLLMs to multilingual contexts.
elements.sent-2411-333: The majority of these efforts involve direct multilingual instruction tuning, enabling MLLMs to follow instructions in multiple languages \citep{tuning-1,tuning-2,tuning-3,tuning-4,tuning-5,tuning-7,tuning-8,tuning-9,tuning-10,tuning-11,tuning-12,tuning-13,tuning-14,tuning-15 , while others focus on multilingual preference tuning, aligning MLLMs with human preferences across multiple languages \citep{tuning-1,tuning-6,tuning-12 .}}
elements.sent-2411-334: These studies adopt significantly different approaches to collecting multilingual tuning data.
elements.sent-2411-335: Additionally, the research question of how to effectively stimulate cross-lingual transfer during the multilingual tuning process is widely discussed.
elements.sent-2411-336: Expanding the tuning of MLLMs into multilingual scenarios necessitates the collection of multilingual tuning data.
elements.sent-2411-337: Here, we'll outline this process separately for multilingual instruction tuning and multilingual preference tuning.

elements.sent-2411-357: In the process of direct multilingual tuning, effectively stimulating cross-lingual transfer under limited resources is an important research question \citep{xu2023language .}
elements.sent-2411-358: Here, we introduce how this challenge is addressed in terms of multilingual instruction tuning and multilingual preference tuning, respectively.

elements.subsection-2411-011: Multilingual Tuning Augmented by Cross-Lingual Alignment
elements.sent-2411-373: While direct multilingual tuning can enhance the multilingual capabilities of MLLMs to some extent, many approaches augment multilingual tuning with cross-lingual alignment to further improve cross-lingual transfer \citep{li2024improving,peng2024concept .}
elements.sent-2411-374: They typically use English, the best resourced language, as the pivot language, aligning the understanding, reasoning, and generation capabilities of MLLMs in non-English languages with those in English.
elements.sent-2411-375: Based on how cross-lingual signals are incorporated to achieve alignment, these works can be classified into two categories: Translation-Assisted Tuning and Cross-Lingual Tuning.
elements.sent-2411-376: Specifically, Translation-Assisted Tuning defines auxiliary translation-related tasks and combines it with original multilingual tuning, while Cross-Lingual Tuning transforms multilingual tuning tasks into cross-lingual forms without explicitly relying on translation task.
elements.sent-2411-377: It's worth noting that multilingual tuning here generally refers to multilingual instruction tuning, as most approaches perform cross-lingual alignment at this stage.
elements.sent-2411-378: The only exception is the work done by \citet{trans-10 , which models cross-lingual alignment as a preference optimization problem.}
elements.sent-2411-379: Assuming the internal translation capability of MLLMs can facilitate cross-lingual alignment, Translation-Assisted Tuning methods define auxiliary translation-related tasks and combine them with original multilingual tuning.
elements.sent-2411-380: Such tasks involve machine translation \citep{trans-1, trans-2, trans-6, trans-5 or other variations \citep{trans-3, trans-4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-381: The most straightforward approach is to convert parallel data into translation instruction format and incorporate them to the original multilingual instruction data.
elements.sent-2411-382: While \citet{trans-1 focused on bilingual scenarios, \citet{trans-2 initially explored the correlation between the scale of translation instructions and translation performance through bilingual training, which subsequently served as a reference for determining the allocation of translation instructions in multilingual settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-383: Specifically, for multilingual reasoning tasks, \citet{trans-5 proposed translation instruction tuning of parallel reasoning questions (Question Alignment) before English instruction tuning for reasoning tasks, thereby facilitating the transfer of English reasoning capabilities of MLLMs to non-English languages.}
elements.sent-2411-384: Similarly, starting with Question Alignment, \citet{trans-6 further integrated general multilingual translation instructions (from English to non-English) with English instruction tuning for reasoning tasks, enhancing the ability of MLLMs to generate multilingual reasoning outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-385: Other studies explore variations of traditional machine translation tasks and transform them into instruction tuning formats.
elements.sent-2411-386: Specifically, \citet{trans-3 extended translation instruction tuning to a multi-turn interactive setting, aiming to simultaneously enhance the cross-lingual alignment and instruction-following capabilities of MLLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-387: Additionally, \citet{trans-4 introduced a cross-lingual semantic similarity task, where LLMs are trained to determine the semantic relationship between two parallel sentences.}
elements.sent-2411-388: They also introduced a bilingual denoising task, training MLLMs to reconstruct the target side of input parallel sentences that have been noised.
elements.sent-2411-389: Without relying on auxiliary translation-related tasks, the approaches in Cross-Lingual Tuning reconstruct multilingual instruction tuning into a cross-lingual form, enhancing the cross-lingual alignment of MLLMs during instruction learning in a more integrated manner \citep{si2024mpn .}
elements.sent-2411-390: In \citep{trans-7 , cross-lingual signals are introduced by specifying instructions in non-English languages and responses in English.}
elements.sent-2411-391: Certain tokens in the non-English instructions may be replaced with English to construct code-switched instructions.
elements.sent-2411-392: In \citep{trans-8 and \citep{trans-9 , MLLMs were trained to respond to non-English instructions by first thinking in English and then responding in a non-English language.}}
elements.sent-2411-393: When the input is a non-English instruction, MLLMs are trained to sequentially output the English translation of the instruction, an English response, and finally a non-English response.
elements.sent-2411-394: The Random Online CoT introduced by \citet{trans-7 serves a similar purpose.}
elements.sent-2411-395: Other works promote cross-lingual alignment by enhancing the cross-lingual consistency of model responses.
elements.sent-2411-396: When responses are required to be in English, the Cross-lingual Distillation method introduced by \citet{trans-7 minimizes the difference between generated English responses when inputs are in different languages.}
elements.sent-2411-397: For multilingual responses, \citet{trans-10 employed high-performing multilingual translation models (e.g., NLLB-600M-distilled) to compute cross-lingual consistency between non-English and English responses.}
elements.sent-2411-398: They further framed cross-lingual alignment as a preference optimization problem, utilizing the computed cross-lingual consistency as rewards for preference tuning.
elements.sent-2411-399: The direct multilingual tuning and cross-lingual alignment discussed above serve as foundational strategies for enhancing MLLMs' capabilities in multilingual instruction following, preference alignment and complex reasoning \citep{zhao2024adamergex .}
elements.sent-2411-400: Beyond this, there is interest in more multilingual abilities of MLLMs, including diverse capabilities of understanding and generation.
elements.sent-2411-401: Those specific multilingual abilities can be enhanced using particular techniques, which we will address here.
elements.sent-2411-402: We will focus on three of such abilities: adaptation to new languages, machine translation and cultural adaptation, as they inherently involve multiple languages or cultures.
elements.sent-2411-403: Adaptation to new languages primarily involves adapting English-centric MLLMs to other languages; machine translation focuses on translating between different languages; and cultural adaptation addresses the cultural diversity of our society, with language considered one of the proxies of culture.
elements.sent-2411-404: Although the techniques employed for adaptation to new languages are general, primarily focusing on vocabulary expansion and continual pre-training, the target languages for adaptation vary across different works \citep{adapt-1, adapt-2, adapt-3, adapt-4, adapt-5 .}
elements.sent-2411-405: For instance, \citet{adapt-1 aimed to extend English-based LLMs to Chinese.}
elements.sent-2411-406: To enhance the efficiency of MLLMs in encoding and decoding Chinese text, they trained an additional tokenizer on Chinese corpora before conducting continual pre-training, expanding the original vocabulary of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-407: Other studies extend the vocabulary and perform continual pre-training on different target languages, such as Korean \citep{adapt-2 , Japanese \citep{adapt-3 , or multiple languages \citep{adapt-4, adapt-5 , and so forth.}}}
elements.sent-2411-408: Additionally, \citet{adapt-2 proposed a seven-stage continuous pre-training strategy to enable LLMs to better learn new token representations.}
elements.sent-2411-409: Adaptation to new languages presents a significant challenge when data for the target languages are not available.
elements.sent-2411-410: In one such case, to enable monolingual instruction-tuned MLLMs to perform multilingual mathematical reasoning tasks, \citet{adapt-6 employed an external multilingual encoder and initialized a trainable linear layer as a bridge between the encoder and the MLLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-411: They enhanced them with multilingual understanding capabilities by aligning the representation space of this encoder and the MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-412: In another case, considering the high computational and data resource costs of the strategy of first expanding the vocabulary and then performing continual pre-training, \citet{adapt-7 advocated for more efficient reuse of the original vocabulary rather than for expanding it.}

elements.sent-2411-414: Factors considered in this romanization scheme include the similarity between the romanized text and the typical writing style, compatibility with the original LLM tokenizer, and the lossiness of the conversion process.
elements.sent-2411-415: Other studies conduct comparative analyses of key techniques for adapting to new languages \citep{adapt-8, adapt-9, adapt-10 .}
elements.sent-2411-416: Specifically, \citet{adapt-8 compared three languages adaptation strategies, viz.}

elements.sent-2411-419: They also gathered empirical evidence that training from a base model rather than an instruction-tuned model could result in better languages adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-420: Many works use MLLMs to implements machine translation task \citep{zhu2024towards,zhu2024feds,cui2024efficiently,zhu2024landermt,chen2024dual,zhang2024paying .}
elements.sent-2411-421: To adapt MLLMs to machine translation, \citet{mt-1 utilized extensive parallel corpora (exceeding 300M) for fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-422: However, it has been observed that using excessive parallel data (e.g., 5M or 20M) for translation task training on well pre-trained MLLMs can have adverse effects, potentially due to catastrophic forgetting \citep{mt-2 .}
elements.sent-2411-423: To address this issue, \citet{mt-2 proposed a two-stage fine-tuning approach.}
elements.sent-2411-424: Initially, they conducted continual pre-training on the languages involved in the translation task to enhance MLLMs' proficiency in those languages.
elements.sent-2411-425: Then, they performed translation instruction tuning on a small scale (e.g., 58K) but with high-quality parallel data.
elements.sent-2411-426: Other studies \citep{mt-3, mt-4 conducted detailed analyses on multilingual translation instruction tuning, taking into account factors such as language similarity, the volume of pre-training and instruction tuning data, and the translation directions.}
elements.sent-2411-427: Although commonly used to enhance MLLMs' translation capabilities, translation instruction tuning is regarded as having certain limitations.
elements.sent-2411-428: On the one hand, the quality of translation instruction data can restrict the performance of MLLMs \citep{mt-5 .}
elements.sent-2411-429: On the other hand, MLLMs fine-tuned with translation instructions may overlook specific contextual knowledge, forget instructions, and consequently encounter issues such as hallucinations, over-translation or translation omissions \citep{mt-6, mt-7, mt-8 .}
elements.sent-2411-430: To address these challenges, some efforts have opted for preference tuning to further enhance the translation capabilities of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-431: To build preference pairs, \citet{mt-5 employed different MLLMs to sample translation results, which were then scored by reference-free evaluation models.}
elements.sent-2411-432: To efficiently collect high-quality preference data, \citet{mt-9 aligned multilingual versions of books to acquire human translations, forming preference pairs alongside machine-generated translations.}
elements.sent-2411-433: Additionally, \citet{mt-6 utilized external word aligners to annotate the degree of word alignment for translation candidates, while \citet{mt-7 employed output comparison and preference comparison strategies to construct preference data.}}
elements.sent-2411-434: Instead of performing preference tuning, \citet{mt-8 proposed to strengthen the global representations of instructions, aiming to mitigate MLLMs' tendency to forget instructions.}
elements.sent-2411-435: The lack of cross-lingual alignment may also affect machine translation.
elements.sent-2411-436: To enhance cross-lingual alignment, \citet{mt-10 introduced XConST, which employs Kullback-Leibler (KL) regularization on semantically equivalent parallel sentences.}
elements.sent-2411-437: Furthermore, \citet{mt-11 initially utilized external tools to extract statistical word alignment signals and then trained MLLMs to discern these signals.}
elements.sent-2411-438: Additionally, \citet{mt-13 proposed m3P, a multimodal neural machine translation framework to bridge language disparities through universal visual features.}
elements.sent-2411-439: As research continues to advance MLLMs in their capabilities, assessing and enriching their cultural and value diversity becomes increasingly crucial, especially considering their extensive utilization across diverse populations worldwide \citep{c-1 .}
elements.sent-2411-440: Significant efforts have been dedicated to exploring the multicultural knowledge embedded within LLMs, as well as the extent of their cultural adaptation and alignment \citep{c-3, c-4, c-5, c-6, c-7, c-8, c-9, c-10, c-11, c-12, c-13, c-14, c-15, c-16, c-17, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16019, c-19, c-20, c-21, c-22, c-23, c-24, c-25, c-26, c-27, c-28, c-29, c-30, c-31, c-32, c-33 .}
elements.sent-2411-441: However, efforts to fine-tune MLLMs for cultural adaptation are relatively sparse, or limited to English.
elements.sent-2411-442: Recently, \citet{c-34 introduced CultureLLM, a methodology aimed at instilling MLLMs with cultural differences.}
elements.sent-2411-443: First, they collected seed data from the World Values Survey \citep{wsv , spanning 9 cultures, and utilized GPT-4 for data augmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-444: Subsequently, they fine-tuned LLMs on the augmented data, creating culture-specific models for each culture alongside a unified model.
elements.sent-2411-445: Furthermore, \citet{c-34-1 proposed CultureSPA, a framework that achieves pluralistic cultural alignment in MLLMs by leveraging their internal cultural knowledge.}
elements.sent-2411-446: This framework involves generating diverse questions from seed prompts, yielding both culture-unaware and culture-aware MLLM outputs, collecting culture-related QA pairs and conducting culture-joint and specific SFT.
elements.sent-2411-447: They validated the effectiveness of the method across 18 cultures spanning five continents.

elements.sent-2411-453: The evaluation of MLLMs are crucial to have a better understanding towards their performance and capabilities, being also important to assess human-values compliance and to address safety concerns \citep{zhang2024enhancing,park2024multiprageval,shen2024language,doddapaneni2024cross .}
elements.sent-2411-454: In multilingual contexts, the evaluation should be broaden towards diverse languages, where the performance towards each language should be understandable.
elements.sent-2411-455: In this section, we discuss the multilingual evaluation of MLLMs, focusing on tokenizers, benchmarks and datasets, multilingualism of MLLMs, and MLLMs as multilingual evaluators.
elements.sent-2411-456: The framework in this section is shown in Figure \ref{fig:Multilingual Evaluation of MLLMs .}
elements.sent-2411-457: A tokenizer plays an important role for supporting multilingual processes in LLMs, as tokenizers separate sentences into tokens and map them into numerical ids that are the input for MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-458: Tokenizers that supports multilingual tokens are crucial to enhance the performance of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-459: The fertility of a tokenizer is a metric aimed at assessing its quality, which is defined as the average number of sub-words produced per tokenized word \citep{DBLP:conf/acl/RustPVRG20 .}
elements.sent-2411-460: A lower fertility score indicates a better quality tokenizer.
elements.sent-2411-461: Fertility is also known as the tokenizer's compression rate \citep{xue-etal-2022-byt5 .}
elements.sent-2411-462: Fertility tests have been conducted to evaluate tokenizers on multilingual sentences across various MLLMs, including BLOOM \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2211-05100 and OpenAI models \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/AhujaDHORJNGSAB23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-463: Based on their findings, the fertility score of OpenAI tokenizers is higher in low-resource languages compared to BLOOM's tokenizer.
elements.sent-2411-464: Another way to evaluate a tokenizer is using parity, which is introduced in \citep{DBLP:conf/nips/PetrovMTB23 and it is motivated by the unequal treatment of tokenization across different languages.}
elements.sent-2411-465: For instance, a Japanese kanji character can be tokenized into three tokens in GPT-2.
elements.sent-2411-466: To address this issue, the concept of tokenizer parity is introduced to evaluate how fairly tokenizers treat equivalent sentences in different languages.
elements.sent-2411-467: A tokenizer achieves parity when the ratio of the tokenization results for a sentence in language A compared to a sentence in language B is almost equal to 1.
elements.sent-2411-468: A comprehensive evaluation of tokenizers was undertaken by \citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2310-08754 , dividing the assessment into intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations.}
elements.sent-2411-469: The intrinsic evaluation focuses solely on metrics like fertility and parity.
elements.sent-2411-470: The extrinsic evaluation evaluates the impact of the tokenizer on the performance of LLMs, with respect to downstream tasks performance and computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-471: The research found that tokenizers trained with a balanced share across languages achieve better fertility and parity scores.
elements.sent-2411-472: The impact of worst, higher fertility will increase computational costs.

elements.sent-2411-547: The safety of MLLMs is a major concern before deployment for public use.
elements.sent-2411-548: It is crucial to evaluate their robustness and risk mitigation against adversarial attacks, malicious inputs and the generation of unsafe outputs.

elements.sent-2411-566: Recent approaches to evaluating the multilingual capabilities of MLLMs involve testing them with evaluation datasets and assessing the generated text \citep{zhao2024large,aggarwal2024maple .}
elements.sent-2411-567: However, a deep understanding of MLLMs' multilingualism remains underexplored.

elements.sent-2411-572: To proceed with the analysis, languages were divided into four multilingual quality quadrants based on the results.
elements.sent-2411-573: The selfish quadrant contains languages whose model's capabilities only increase for the corresponding language pair.
elements.sent-2411-574: The reciprocal quadrant have languages whose training with bilingual data improves corresponding language pair and boost multilingual capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-575: The altruistic quadrant encompasses languages that primarily improve the multilingual performance.
elements.sent-2411-576: The idle quadrant contain languages that do not improve neither bilingual nor multilingual performance.
