elements.sent-2411-588: Training an MLLM with multilingual data and subsequently fine-tuning it with multilingual instruction data can result in a highly effective MLLM.
elements.sent-2411-589: However, understanding the underlying mechanisms of such a model becomes challenging due to the diverse distribution of the training data and the complexity of the model's structure.
elements.sent-2411-590: Exploring the interpretability of MLLMs, or just MLLMs in general, is crucial for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-591: When processing sensitive information or making important decisions, it is essential to explain the model's behavior and inferential processes to ensure compliance with legal, ethical or social standards.
elements.sent-2411-592: Due to imbalances in the quantity and quality of linguistic data, MLLMs may perform better in some languages than others, leading to linguistic bias.
elements.sent-2411-593: By interpreting the models' internal decision-making processes, we can better identify and correct these biases, making the models fairer.
elements.sent-2411-594: For instance, understanding the mechanisms behind cross-lingual transfer in MLLMs can help enhance the performance of low-resource languages.
elements.sent-2411-595: While the interpretability of MLLMs is challenging, the interpretability of MLLMs brings specific complexities.
elements.sent-2411-596: It centers on how the unique attributes of various languages and their interactions influence these models.
elements.sent-2411-597: Our discussion on the interpretability of MLLMs will begin by exploring three main issues: how these models manage multilingual capabilities, how they perform cross-lingual transfer, and the reasons behind language bias.
elements.sent-2411-598: The first issue is the basis for investigating the interpretability of MLLMs, while the latter two issues are phenomena that arise after modeling multilingual capabilities in MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-599: The framework of this section is shown in Figure \ref{fig:Interpretabilities of MLLMs , and the specific perspectives and approaches are shown in Figure \ref{fig:Multilingual LLMs Interpretability Perspectives and Approaches .}}
elements.sent-2411-600: Studying the interpretability of MLLMs first requires understanding how these models specifically handle multilingual capabilities, how inputs from different languages are represented and processed \citep{zhao2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-601: This involves examining how the model shares semantic information across languages and manages language-specific features.
elements.sent-2411-602: Previous studies have explored the multilingual capabilities of MLLMs through four perspectives: model, component, neuron and representation.
elements.sent-2411-603: At the model level, the behavior and decision paths of the entire model is analyzed to understand its overall multilingual processing capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-604: At the component level, the focus is on the internal components, such as feed-forward network and attention module, and investigates their roles in a multilingual environment.
elements.sent-2411-605: The neuron level delves into finer granularity, exploring the functions of individual neurons in multilingual tasks.
elements.sent-2411-606: Finally, the representation level examines how the model learns multilingual representations, investigating how inputs from different languages are represented and distinguished in high-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-607: Through these levels of analysis, a comprehensive understanding is gained on how MLLMs operate and process multilingual information.
elements.sent-2411-608: To uncover the internal mechanisms of MLLMs modeling multilingual capabilities, some studies begins by examining the model as a whole.
elements.sent-2411-609: Generally, this aims to divide the model's processing of multilingual data into distinct stages.
elements.sent-2411-610: Specifically, these studies are categorized into two perspectives: static reasoning and dynamic training.
elements.sent-2411-611: Static reasoning focuses on the trained model, while dynamic training examines the model during its training process.
elements.sent-2411-612: Existing studies suggest that the layers closest to a model's input or output exhibit more language-specific behaviors than the layers in the middle \citep{DBLP:conf/blackboxnlp/BhattacharyaB23 .}
elements.sent-2411-613: For instance, \citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-18815 summarized the multilingual workflow of an MLLM by observing changes in the ratio of languages between layers.}
elements.sent-2411-614: In this workflow, multilingual inputs are converted to English near the input layer.
elements.sent-2411-615: The model uses English for processing and integrates multilingual knowledge through specific structures in the intermediate layers.
elements.sent-2411-616: Near the output layer, the information is converted back to the original input language.
elements.sent-2411-617: Additionally, some studies interpret the intermediate process as encoding into a conceptual space, noting that the model appears to think in concepts that favor English, as the English space seems to be closer to this conceptual space than other languages spaces \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-10588 .}
elements.sent-2411-618: Compared to monolingual LLMs, MLLMs acquire information within each language in essentially the same order as monolingual models.
elements.sent-2411-619: However, this internal language learning occurs early in the training process.
elements.sent-2411-620: In contrast, cross-lingual information transfer is learned throughout pre-training, with the sequence of transferring linguistic information between specific languages varying considerably.
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{center} \includegraphics[scale=0.8]{latex/interpretability.pdf} \caption{MLLMs interpretability perspectives and approaches.} \label{fig:Multilingual LLMs Interpretability Perspectives and Approaches} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-621: The components in MLLMs play a crucial role in modeling multilingual capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-622: In \citet{DBLP:conf/blackboxnlp/BhattacharyaB23 , distinct patterns of multilingual processing in the sub-layers of the model's feed-forward network were discovered.}
elements.sent-2411-623: It divided FFNs into detectors and combiners, finding that the detectors in the early and late layers are multilingual, while the combiners in the middle layer also exhibit multilingual properties.
elements.sent-2411-624: Due to the complexity of MLLM structures, where each component can have multiple functions, numerous studies have investigated how MLLMs model multilingual capabilities at a finer granularity--—the neuron level.
elements.sent-2411-625: A core region corresponding to multilingual capabilities in MLLMs was identified in \citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-14700 , as perturbing parameters in this region were demonstrated to affect performance across all languages.}
elements.sent-2411-626: Additionally, it found a region related to specific monolingual capabilities, where altering parameters decreases the performance of the corresponding language.
elements.sent-2411-627: Another perspective differentiates between language-agnostic neurons, responsible for processing generalized knowledge, and language-specific neurons, which handle language-specific vocabulary, grammar, and idiomatic expressions \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-16438, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-16367, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-02431 .}
elements.sent-2411-628: Considering interpretability from the perspective of model-specific structures, including the overall model, components and neurons, is one of the research approaches.
elements.sent-2411-629: Another approach focuses solely on the intermediate representations produced by the model.
elements.sent-2411-630: This involves transforming high-dimensional model representations into low-dimensional, human-understandable features through specific operations, typically probe methods.
elements.sent-2411-631: These probe methods can be divided into two categories: parameter-free probing methods, such as direct feature dimensionality reduction; and parametric probing methods, such as those using diagnostic classifiers to classify features.
elements.sent-2411-632: The parameter-free probing approach primarily targets encoder-based MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-633: Studies have shown that after applying mean-centering operations on the representations of MLLMs, different languages occupy similar linear subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-634: Additionally, it has been found that these MLLMs encode information along orthogonal axes that are either language-sensitive or language-neutral, addressing both language-agnostic and language-specific information \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/ChangTB22 .}
elements.sent-2411-635: In contrast, some other studies aim at maximizing the use of language-agnostic information in representations while ignoring language-specific information.
elements.sent-2411-636: This is achieved through methods like clustering-based anisotropy enhancement \citep{DBLP:conf/acl/RajaeeP22 or identifying low-rank subspaces that encode semantically irrelevant information \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/XieZ0L22 , which can improve the performance of language-agnostic tasks, such as cross-lingual text similarity \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/TiyajamornKAO21 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-637: Other studies have explored the reason of cross-lingual generic representations, identifying the significant role of shared parameters.
elements.sent-2411-638: Surprisingly, these studies also found similarities in the representations of models from different languages even when parameters are not shared \citep{DBLP:conf/acl/ConneauWLZS20 .}
elements.sent-2411-639: Some parametric probing methods focus on uncovering the underlying cross-lingual syntactic and morphological representations.
elements.sent-2411-640: By applying probing classifiers to syntax and morphology-related tasks, researchers have found that MLLMs learn syntactic and morphological content in different languages in a very similar way \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2104-12847 .}
elements.sent-2411-641: These models can even learn this content across languages in a monolingual-like manner \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/StaracePCPRLS23 .}
elements.sent-2411-642: The intrinsic probing approach suggests this may be because some neurons can encode universal syntactic and morphological information.
elements.sent-2411-643: However, since subsets of neurons from different languages do not completely overlap, the complexity of syntactic morphological information and the distance between languages may affect the learning of syntactic morphology across languages \citep{DBLP:conf/naacl/StanczakPHCA22 .}
elements.sent-2411-644: In more advanced research, some approaches explore lexical and semantic representations in MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-645: Cross-lingual lexical fine-tuning followed by interpolation with static linguistic word embeddings was used in \citet{DBLP:conf/eacl/VulicGLCPK23 to reveal cross-lingual lexical knowledge from MLLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-646: Additionally, \citet{DBLP:conf/coling/VardaM24 trained linear probes using extracted representations to identify neurons most relevant to the target lexicon, discovering significant cross-lingual overlap in these neurons.}
elements.sent-2411-647: A special case of lexical representation is factual knowledge representation.
elements.sent-2411-648: Existing MLLMs lack cross-lingual consistency in encoding factual knowledge, leading to different answers when the same factual question is asked in different languages \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/JiangAADN20, DBLP:conf/eacl/KassnerDS21, DBLP:conf/emnlp/QiFB23 .}
elements.sent-2411-649: One potential solution is to explicitly transfer relatively rich factual knowledge from English to non-English languages \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/XuLX23 .}
elements.sent-2411-650: Cross-lingual transfer refers to the transfer of knowledge from one language to another.
elements.sent-2411-651: Despite the challenges posed by differences between languages, similar concepts can facilitate this process.
elements.sent-2411-652: Studies have shown that zero-shot and few-shot cross-lingual transfer occurs in MLLMs \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/XuZYZH23 .}
elements.sent-2411-653: While some attribute this phenomenon to shared sub-word tokens \citep{DBLP:conf/naacl/DeshpandeTN22 , others argue that parameter sharing \citep{DBLP:conf/acl/ConneauWLZS20 and network depth \citep{DBLP:conf/iclr/KWMR20 are more crucial.}}}
elements.sent-2411-654: Additionally, language-agnostic neurons \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-14700, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-16367 and language-agnostic representations \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/ChangTB22 in MLLMs may play a key role, storing or representing cross-lingual semantic knowledge and enabling conceptual alignment between languages.}}
elements.sent-2411-655: Language bias refers to the tendency to favor or discriminate against certain groups in language use \citep{zhang2024getting .}
elements.sent-2411-656: This issue arises in MLLMs due to the imbalance in the distribution of high- and low-resource languages within their training data.
elements.sent-2411-657: Consequently, MLLMs can exhibit language bias, such as not fully understanding the cultural context of certain languages.
elements.sent-2411-658: This bias may stem from the distribution imbalance between high- and low-resource datasets, causing the model to shift inputs from various languages to high-resource languages, particularly English, to process information \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-18815 .}
elements.sent-2411-659: Additionally, it has been suggested that this bias could be due to the close proximity between the space of English tokens and abstract concepts, further contributing to linguistic bias in MLLMs \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-10588 .}

elements.sent-2411-660: Due to its excellent multi-language understanding and cross-language generalization capabilities, MLLMs have demonstrated excellent performance in various downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-661: Therefore, MLLM is widely used in various professional domains, including biology and medicine, computer science, mathematics, law, etc. These domain-specific MLLMs have demonstrated outstanding capabilities and promising perspectives in related domains, and have even surpassed human levels in some aspects.
elements.sent-2411-662: As a consequence, MLLMs provide a new approach for the integration of artificial intelligence and these domains.
elements.sent-2411-663: Nevertheless, the application of MLLMs in these domains remains challenging, mainly due to the reliance on specific expertise and data collection requirements.
elements.sent-2411-664: At present, MLLMs abound more for English and Chinese, and there are fewer suitable for low-resource languages, which greatly hinders the development of generative AI on a global scale.
elements.sent-2411-665: In this section, we address the development trajectory and recent progress of MLLMs across different domains, focusing on their practical applications, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:Application of MLLMs .}
elements.sent-2411-666: The integration of MLLMs has shown tremendous potential in the health sector, particularly in applications such as medical Q\&A, intelligent diagnosis, and psychological counseling \citep{qiu2024towards,lifelo2024adapting,garcia2024medical .}

elements.sent-2411-692: Recently, there have been significant advancements in the application of large language models within the domain of computer science, particularly in tasks such as code generation and text-to-SQL conversion.
elements.sent-2411-693: The application of large language models is reshaping the domain of computer science.
elements.sent-2411-694: The paradigm has shifted from manually writing code to generating it and making human corrections.
elements.sent-2411-695: In this subsection, we will explore the development history and recent advancements of MLLMs in the domain of computer science.
elements.sent-2411-696: Encoder-Only: Due to the Encoder-Only architecture's ability to effectively capture the global dependencies and features of input sequences, it excels in code detection and classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-697: This model comprises 124 million parameters and supports 6 different programming languages.
elements.sent-2411-698: In the same year, CuBERT \citep{DBLP:conf/icml/KanadeMBS20 was introduced, focusing on training BERT on source code to obtain contextual embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-699: This model was used to identify code block defects and detect duplicate code blocks.
elements.sent-2411-700: Encoder-Decoder: The Encoder-Decoder architecture is a neural network model widely used in natural language processing and other sequence-to-sequence tasks.
elements.sent-2411-701: It has now been extensively applied to tasks such as text generation, question answering, and code generation.
elements.sent-2411-702: PLBART \citep{DBLP:conf/naacl/AhmadCRC21 adopts the encoder-decoder BART architecture and is pre-trained on an extensive collection of programming languages (PL) and natural languages (NL) via denoising autoencoding.}
elements.sent-2411-703: It is primarily used for code summarization, text-to-code generation, code-to-code translation, and code refinement.
elements.sent-2411-704: CodeT5 \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/0034WJH21 supports eight common programming languages and employs the same pre-training approach as T5 \citep{DBLP:journals/jmlr/RaffelSRLNMZLL20 .}}
elements.sent-2411-705: It focuses on masked span prediction, denoising sequence reconstruction and masked identifier prediction with a bimodal dual generation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-706: This strategy encourages better alignment between NL and PL, allowing CodeT5 to significantly outperform PLBART across all generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-707: CodeT5+ \citep{DBLP:conf/emnlp/WangLGB0H23 is an enhanced version of CodeT5, employing a larger parameter scale to deliver enhanced performance and more accurate code comprehension.}
elements.sent-2411-708: It mainly features three parameter levels: 2B, 6B and 16B.
elements.sent-2411-709: Across various code-related tasks such as code completion, code recommendation and code classification, it demonstrates superior performance compared to CodeT5.
elements.sent-2411-710: In 2022, DeepMind announced the launch of AlphaCode \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2203-07814 , a code generation system that can create novel solutions to problems requiring deeper reasoning, using a Transformer-based model.}
elements.sent-2411-711: It was trained on over 715 GB of data from GitHub and Codeforces issues and solutions, and it supports twelve of the most common programming languages.
elements.sent-2411-712: Decoder-Only: The Decoder-Only architecture is currently the most commonly used MLLM architecture, typically employed for sequence generation tasks such as text generation and machine translation.
elements.sent-2411-713: Codex \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2107-03374 , a model provided exclusively through OpenAI's API, is a descendant of GPT-3.}
elements.sent-2411-714: It is available in three sizes: 300M, 2.5B and 12B, and it powers GitHub Copilot, a well-known and robust model renowned for its performance.
elements.sent-2411-715: While excelling in Python, it also demonstrates proficiency in a variety of other programming languages, for tasks such as code translation, code explanation and code refactoring.
elements.sent-2411-716: However, unlike other models, Codex is not publicly downloadable.
elements.sent-2411-717: PolyCoder \citep{DBLP:conf/pldi/Xu0NH22 is an MLLM based on the GPT-2 architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-718: It has been trained on 249GB of code across twelve common programming languages and is available in three sizes: 160M, 0.4B and 2.7B.
elements.sent-2411-719: In the C programming language, PolyCoder outperforms all previous models, including Codex.
elements.sent-2411-720: CodeParrot is a GPT-2 model trained exclusively on 180GB of Python code.
elements.sent-2411-721: It is available in two sizes, 110M and 1.5B parameters.
elements.sent-2411-722: CodeGen \citep{DBLP:conf/iclr/NijkampPHTWZSX23 is an autoregressive language model designed for program synthesis, sequentially trained on The Pile, BigQuery and BigPython datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-723: Its goal is to enhance developer productivity by converting metadata into readable and maintainable source code.
elements.sent-2411-724: Incoder \citep{DBLP:conf/iclr/FriedAL0WSZYZL23 is trained on code using a causal masking objective, enabling code insertion/filling as well as standard left-to-right generation.}
elements.sent-2411-725: It is trained on public open-source repositories with permissive, non-Copyleft licenses from GitHub, GitLab, and StackOverflow.
elements.sent-2411-726: These repositories predominantly contain Python and JavaScript but also include code in twenty eight other languages sourced from StackOverflow.
elements.sent-2411-727: Incoder is available in two variants, 1.3B and 6B parameters.
elements.sent-2411-728: Codey, a fine-tuned model of PaLM2,\footnote{https://lablab.ai/tech/google/codey\#codey-google-ais-revolutionary-coding-assistant is capable of performing varying coding tasks.}

elements.sent-2411-730: Google claims that Codey can code in more than twenty programming languages.
elements.sent-2411-731: It is used to enhance Google products like Google Colab, Android Studio, and more.
elements.sent-2411-732: The CodeLlama \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2308-12950 release introduces a series of models with 7B, 13B and 34B parameters.}

elements.sent-2411-734: Meta subsequently fine-tuned these base models into two distinct flavors: a Python specialist variant (with an additional 100B tokens) and an instruction fine-tuned version capable of comprehending natural language instructions.
elements.sent-2411-735: These models exhibit state-of-the-art performance across various programming languages.
elements.sent-2411-736: StarCoder \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2305-06161 is trained on permissively licensed data from GitHub, encompassing over eighty programming languages, Git commits, GitHub issues and Jupyter notebooks.}

elements.sent-2411-738: StarCode outperforms existing open code language models on popular programming benchmarks and matches or surpasses closed models such as Codex.
elements.sent-2411-739: ChatGPT and GPT-4 are advanced language models developed by OpenAI.
elements.sent-2411-740: They utilize Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) to enhance their program synthesis capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-741: These models have demonstrated proficiency in code generation tasks, often surpassing human-level performance.

elements.sent-2411-744: Leveraging the superior performance of ChatGLM2, CodeGeeX2 achieves performance improvements across multiple benchmarks.

elements.sent-2411-747: It underwent cold start training on 500B tokens, with a context window length of 8192.
elements.sent-2411-748: CodeShell's code generation performance surpasses that of CodeLlama-7B and StarCoder-7B.
elements.sent-2411-749: CodeGemma \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2403-08295 is a family of code-specialist LLM models by Google, based on the pre-trained 2B and 7B Gemma \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2403-08295 checkpoints.}}
elements.sent-2411-750: CodeGemma are further trained on an additional 500B tokens of primarily English language data, mathematics and code to improve on logical and mathematical reasoning, and are suitable for code completion and generation.
elements.sent-2411-751: CodeQwen1.5 \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-16609 is the code-specific version of Qwen1.5, pre-trained on a vast corpus of code data.}
elements.sent-2411-752: With a context length of 64K tokens and support for ninety two programming languages, it exhibits robust code generation capabilities across a range of benchmark tests.
elements.sent-2411-753: Additionally, it demonstrates outstanding performance in tasks such as text-to-SQL conversion and error correction.
elements.sent-2411-754: In recent years, there has been a significant surge in the development of large language models aimed at automating the process of solving mathematical problems, which given the wide-ranging and diverse nature of mathematical problem, presents a challenge to the development of this emerging field.
elements.sent-2411-755: Multilingual large language models perform exceptionally well in tasks such as text completion and machine translation.
elements.sent-2411-756: However, they exhibit notable limitations in solving, explaining, answering and recommending solutions for mathematical problems.
elements.sent-2411-757: In this subsection, we will discuss recent advancements of MLLMs in the domain of mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-758: MathGPT \citep{scarlatos-lan-2023-tree is a large-scale model targeting global mathematics enthusiasts and research institutions with problem-solving and teaching algorithms at its core.}
elements.sent-2411-759: Its mathematical computation capabilities span across primary, middle and high school levels.
elements.sent-2411-760: In evaluations across six publicly available math test sets--—CEval-Math, AGIEval-Math, APE5K, CMMLU-Math, high school math exams, and Math401--—MathGPT has achieved the highest scores in multiple tests, even surpassing those of GPT-4.
elements.sent-2411-761: The ChatGLM-Math \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-02893 model is an LLM that enhances mathematical problem-solving abilities through a self-critical pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-762: This model not only improves mathematical skills but also preserves and enhances language capabilities, resulting in performance enhancements across various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-763: The DeepSeekMath \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-03300 model underwent pretraining with a total of 500 billion tokens, which included mathematical-related texts from Common Crawl as well as natural language and code data.}
elements.sent-2411-764: Built upon the foundational architecture of DeepSeek-Coder-v1.5 7B, the model received specialized instruction tuning and reinforcement learning training to enhance its mathematical problem-solving and tool utilization abilities.
elements.sent-2411-765: Additionally, DeepSeekMath 7B achieved performance levels similar to Gemini-Ultra and GPT-4 in the highly competitive MATH Challenge.
elements.sent-2411-766: MetaMath \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-12284 , fine-tuned on the MetaMathQA dataset using the LLaMA-2 architecture, is a large language model specialized in mathematical reasoning (both forward and backward).}
elements.sent-2411-767: MetaMath-70B has surpassed ChatGPT in performance on mathematical reasoning datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results.
elements.sent-2411-768: MAmmoTH \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-05653 underwent instruction fine-tuning on the MathInstruct dataset, which covers various mathematical domains and complexities, blending Concept of Thinking (CoT) with Programming of Thinking (PoT).}
elements.sent-2411-769: The MAmmoTH-34B model has surpassed the CoT results of GPT-4 on competition-level datasets.

elements.sent-2411-771: It surpasses advanced models such as ChatGPT, Claude Instant-1, PaLM-2, and Minerva on the GSM8K dataset.
elements.sent-2411-772: GAIRMath-Abel \citep{abel achieves state-of-the-art performance across open-source LLMs solely through the utilization of Parental Oversight, a Babysitting Strategy for Supervised Fine-tuning, without requiring tools, continued pretraining, reward modeling or reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF).}
elements.sent-2411-773: Orca-Math \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-14830 , a small language model constructed with 700M parameters, is fine-tuned on the Mistral-7B architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-774: It redefines the traditional approach to teaching mathematical word problems through creative synthetic datasets and iterative learning mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-775: Notably, Orca-Math has achieved significant advancements on the GSM8K benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-776: While general multilingual large language models have the capacity to address a broad spectrum of knowledge domains, the legal field is a highly specialized domain that necessitates both utmost accuracy and timely relevance.
elements.sent-2411-777: General MLLMs may be deficient in their grasp of legal statutes, judicial interpretations and nuances particular to specific jurisdictions, thereby leading to imprecise responses or the omission of crucial information.
elements.sent-2411-778: Consequently, the development of specialized legal MLLMs becomes imperative to overcome these limitations and cater to the specific demands of the legal sector.
elements.sent-2411-779: In this section, we address the most recent advancements of MLLMs within the domain of law.
elements.sent-2411-780: LegalBERT \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2010-02559 , based on the BERT model, is the first large-scale model pre-trained on legal texts.}
elements.sent-2411-781: It was trained on the entire Harvard Law School case corpus, which comprises 37GB of legal data and includes 3,446,187 legal decisions from both federal and state courts.
elements.sent-2411-782: The model demonstrates exceptional performance on downstream legal tasks.
elements.sent-2411-783: Lawformer \citep{DBLP:journals/aiopen/XiaoHLTS21 , based on Longformer, is pre-trained on a large-scale corpus of Chinese legal texts.}
elements.sent-2411-784: As the first Chinese legal pre-training model, it does not use standard self-attention but instead combines local sliding windows with a global attention mechanism to capture long-range dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-785: It demonstrates exceptional performance on long-text legal tasks.
elements.sent-2411-786: LawGPT \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2302-05729 builds on existing general-purpose Chinese language models by expanding the vocabulary with legal-specific terms and conducting pre-training on a large-scale corpus of Chinese legal texts.}
elements.sent-2411-787: This enhances the model's foundational semantic understanding in the legal domain.
elements.sent-2411-788: Additionally, LawGPT undergoes instruction fine-tuning on legal domain dialogue question-answering datasets and the Chinese judicial examination dataset, improving its comprehension and execution of legal content.
elements.sent-2411-789: Fudan University has introduced DISC-LawLLM \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-11325 , a large-scale model designed to provide users with professional, intelligent and comprehensive legal services.}
elements.sent-2411-790: It surpasses ChatGPT in performance on the latest legal evaluation benchmark, Lawbench, and is second only to GPT-4.
elements.sent-2411-791: The ChatLaw \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2306-16092 model comes in two versions, one based on the Ziya-13B model and the other on the Anima-33B model.}
elements.sent-2411-792: It is pre-trained on extensive legal dialogue data, including legal news, forums, statutes, judicial interpretations, legal consultations, judicial exam questions and court judgments.
elements.sent-2411-793: ChatLaw demonstrates excellent performance on various legal tasks.
elements.sent-2411-794: LexiLaw \citep{DBLP:conf/sigir/LiACDW0CT23 , built on the ChatGLM-6B architecture, has been fine-tuned on legal domain datasets to enhance its performance and professionalism in providing legal consultation and support.}
elements.sent-2411-795: Lawyer LLaMA \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2305-15062 , based on the LLaMA-13B architecture, underwent continual pretraining on a large-scale legal corpus.}
elements.sent-2411-796: Subsequently, the model was instruction fine-tuned using collected legal data, enabling a deep understanding of common areas in Chinese law, including civil law, criminal law, administrative law, and procedural law.
elements.sent-2411-797: The rapid advancement of MLLMs has opened exciting new avenues for cross-lingual language understanding and generation.
elements.sent-2411-798: However, several critical challenges remain, hindering the full realization of their potential and necessitating further research and development.
elements.sent-2411-799: Democratizing language technology and effectively addressing linguistic diversity represent paramount challenges in MLLM research.
elements.sent-2411-800: Current models often exhibit a significant performance disparity, favoring high-resource languages like English due to data abundance.
elements.sent-2411-801: Bridging this gap requires innovative approaches to cross-lingual transfer learning, enabling efficient knowledge transfer from resource-rich to resource-scarce languages.
elements.sent-2411-802: This involves exploring advanced techniques such as adapter-based transfer, meta-learning for cross-lingual adaptation, and developing robust interlingua representations that capture universal linguistic properties.
elements.sent-2411-803: Furthermore, addressing the scarcity of data in low-resource languages necessitates sophisticated data augmentation and generation methods.
elements.sent-2411-804: Techniques like back-translation, paraphrasing, and leveraging synthetic data generation through controlled text generation can supplement limited datasets, though careful consideration must be given to cultural sensitivity and appropriateness during data generation.
elements.sent-2411-805: Ultimately, democratizing language technology hinges on developing MLLMs that are genuinely inclusive, capable of understanding and generating text across a wide spectrum of languages, regardless of their resource availability.
elements.sent-2411-806: Beyond supporting a multitude of languages, a crucial aspect of linguistic diversity lies in the rich tapestry of language variations and dialects.
elements.sent-2411-807: MLLMs frequently struggle with these nuances, as they deviate significantly from standardized language forms commonly used in training data.
elements.sent-2411-808: Therefore, future research must focus on incorporating dialectal information during both training and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-809: This may involve developing specialized modules within MLLM architectures that are sensitive to dialectal variations or by leveraging techniques like code-switching and dialect adaptation during fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-810: Creating dedicated evaluation benchmarks that accurately assess MLLM performance on dialectal data is also essential, emphasizing aspects such as comprehension, fluency, and cultural relevance.
elements.sent-2411-811: Furthermore, code-switching and language mixing, prevalent phenomena in multilingual communities, pose additional complexities.
elements.sent-2411-812: Effectively handling these intricate language patterns necessitates developing models capable of explicitly recognizing and adapting to code-switching dynamics, potentially through dedicated modules and by training on large-scale code-switching datasets.
elements.sent-2411-813: Developing truly multilingual AI necessitates moving beyond purely linguistic considerations and embracing the rich tapestry of cultural nuances embedded within language.
elements.sent-2411-814: Towards this goal, building culturally-aware and adaptive MLLMs is a critical research frontier.
elements.sent-2411-815: Current MLLMs, often trained predominantly on English data, risk perpetuating cultural biases and failing to capture the diverse perspectives and values reflected in different languages and cultures.
elements.sent-2411-816: Future research must prioritize integrating cultural knowledge into MLLMs, ensuring they are not only multilingual but also multicultural in their understanding.
elements.sent-2411-817: Training on culturally diverse datasets, encompassing various perspectives and worldviews, is essential.
elements.sent-2411-818: This involves carefully curating data that represents different cultural contexts, including literature, social media conversations, and news articles from diverse sources.
elements.sent-2411-819: Moreover, developing evaluation metrics that capture cultural nuances is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-820: These metrics should move beyond simple accuracy and fluency, assessing aspects such as cultural sensitivity, appropriateness, and the ability to adapt communication styles based on cultural context.
elements.sent-2411-821: This shift towards culturally-aware evaluation will encourage the development of MLLMs that are not only proficient in multiple languages but also respectful and understanding of the diverse cultures they represent.
elements.sent-2411-822: Adaptability is another key dimension of culturally-aware MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-823: Language is not static; it evolves and adapts to changing cultural contexts.
elements.sent-2411-824: Therefore, MLLMs should be equipped to learn and adapt to these evolving cultural dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-825: This involves developing models that can dynamically adjust their understanding and generation based on cultural cues, potentially by incorporating mechanisms for continuous learning and adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-826: For example, an adaptive MLLM could learn to recognize and respond appropriately to culturally-specific humor, idioms, and social norms.
elements.sent-2411-827: Furthermore, research should explore methods for personalizing MLLMs to individual cultural preferences, enabling users to tailor the model's behavior to align with their own cultural background and values.
elements.sent-2411-828: This personalization can enhance user experience and promote inclusivity, ensuring that MLLMs are accessible and beneficial to diverse cultural communities worldwide.
elements.sent-2411-829: Building culturally-aware and adaptive MLLMs requires a multidisciplinary approach, drawing on insights from linguistics, anthropology, sociology, and other fields to develop models that are truly representative of the rich diversity of human culture.
elements.sent-2411-830: Ensuring safety, fairness, and interpretability in MLLMs is paramount, especially as these powerful models become increasingly integrated into diverse real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-831: Their widespread deployment necessitates a rigorous focus on responsible AI principles to mitigate potential risks and ensure ethical and equitable outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-832: Safety, in the context of MLLMs, encompasses safeguarding against adversarial attacks, malicious inputs, and the generation of unsafe or harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-833: This requires developing robust defense mechanisms, including techniques like adversarial training and input sanitization, to enhance MLLM resilience against malicious exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-834: Furthermore, establishing clear safety guidelines and protocols for MLLM deployment is crucial, outlining best practices for data handling, model training, and user interaction.
elements.sent-2411-835: These guidelines should address potential risks associated with specific application domains, such as healthcare, finance, and legal services, ensuring that MLLMs are used responsibly and ethically.
elements.sent-2411-836: Fairness is another critical concern, particularly given the potential for MLLMs to perpetuate or amplify existing societal biases.
elements.sent-2411-837: Bias can manifest in various forms, including gender bias, racial bias, and cultural bias, leading to discriminatory or unfair outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-838: Addressing this challenge requires a multi-pronged approach, encompassing both data and model-level interventions.
elements.sent-2411-839: Developing methods for detecting and mitigating biases in training data, such as debiasing techniques and data augmentation strategies, is essential.
elements.sent-2411-840: Furthermore, incorporating fairness metrics into evaluation frameworks can help quantify and monitor bias in MLLM outputs, encouraging the development of fairer and more equitable models.
elements.sent-2411-841: Promoting fairness also necessitates fostering responsible data collection and annotation practices, ensuring that data used to train MLLMs is representative and inclusive of diverse populations.
elements.sent-2411-842: Interpretability and explainability are crucial for building trust and ensuring accountability in MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-843: The complex nature of these models, often referred to as ``black boxes" makes it challenging to understand their internal decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-844: Enhancing interpretability involves developing techniques for visualizing cross-lingual representations, analyzing attention patterns, and providing human-understandable explanations for MLLM outputs.
elements.sent-2411-845: This requires moving beyond simply generating text to providing insights into how the model arrived at a particular conclusion or prediction.
elements.sent-2411-846: For example, methods like probing tasks and attention visualization can help reveal the underlying linguistic knowledge and reasoning processes employed by MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-847: Furthermore, research should focus on developing methods for explaining cross-lingual transfer, providing insights into how knowledge is transferred between languages and how this transfer can be optimized for improved performance.
elements.sent-2411-848: Ultimately, achieving interpretability and explainability in MLLMs is essential for promoting transparency, fostering trust, and enabling effective human-model collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-849: The computational demands of MLLMs present significant challenges to their widespread accessibility and sustainability.
elements.sent-2411-850: Training and deploying these massive models require substantial resources, raising concerns about their environmental impact and limiting their use by researchers and practitioners with constrained budgets.
elements.sent-2411-851: Addressing this challenge necessitates a multifaceted approach focusing on efficiency improvements across various dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-852: Research should prioritize developing more efficient training algorithms, including techniques like sparse training, pruning, and quantization, as well as exploring lightweight model architectures and model compression methods such as knowledge distillation.
elements.sent-2411-853: Furthermore, investigating hardware acceleration strategies, including specialized hardware and distributed training, can significantly reduce computational costs.
